topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a monotonous tune, mirroring the dull ache settling in my chest. I stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming before my eyes, each figure a tiny, sharp barb. Sarah, bless her oblivious heart, had just casually mentioned her recent bonus structure during a team lunch. The way she spoke about her compensation—confident, unburdened—felt like a physical slap. I traced the line item for my own salary, the contrast between my modest figure and hers widening into an unbearable chasm. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palm, and took a long, slow sip, the bitterness doing little to soothe the tight knot in my stomach. I decided, right then, that I needed to start working twice as hard, just to prove something to myself, even if the effort felt hollow.

[story 2]

I watched Mark stride past my cubicle, the effortless confidence radiating off him like heat. He was discussing a recent project win with the manager, his voice smooth and assured. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly as I watched him receive a nod of approval—a nod that seemed to validate every choice he’d made. I leaned back in my chair, the worn vinyl groaning under my weight. My fingers drummed a frantic rhythm on the keyboard, the clicks sounding too loud in the sudden quiet of my focus. It wasn't just the money; it was the implication. He seemed to glide through the company, while I felt stuck in the slow lane, constantly having to prove my worth with painstaking, almost desperate, detail. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I quickly averted my gaze, focusing instead on the meaningless data on my screen.

[story 3]

The email landed in my inbox, stark and undeniable. The figures were laid out plainly, a brutal comparison that stripped away all pretense. My breath hitched. I reread the details, the stark reality of the disparity sinking in with sickening clarity. A strange, cold emptiness settled over me, a vacuum where motivation used to reside. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly across the floor. I walked to the window, looking out at the city below, feeling utterly insignificant. Every small achievement I’d made in the last year seemed to shrink under the weight of this new knowledge. I turned back to my desk, my hands shaking slightly as I tried to force my fingers to type, the action feeling clumsy and inadequate, a betrayal of the sudden, heavy feeling in my gut.
